---
title: "Git & Version Control"
subtitle: "Track every change, undo mistakes, and collaborate with confidence. The safety net every DH project needs."
author: "Curt Rode"
date: last-modified
difficulty: intermediate
time-estimate: "50 min"
format:
  html:
    code-tools: false
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
---

::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"}
## Learning Objectives
By the end of this guide, you will be able to:

- Explain why version control matters for digital humanities work
- Set up and configure Git on your computer
- Use the core Git workflow: add, commit, push, pull
- Connect a local repository to GitHub
- Recover from common mistakes using Git
:::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
## Why This Matters When You're Working with AI
AI tools can generate code, suggest changes, and refactor your work — but they can also break things. Git is your undo button. When an AI-suggested change goes wrong, `git diff` shows you exactly what changed and `git restore` takes you back. Without version control, you're one bad suggestion away from losing hours of work.
:::

You've been here before: `final_paper.docx`, `final_paper_v2.docx`, `final_paper_FINAL.docx`, `final_paper_FINAL_really_final.docx`. Version control solves this problem permanently. Git tracks every change you make, lets you rewind to any previous version, and makes collaboration possible without emailing files back and forth.

## Why Version Control Matters for DH

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="vc-motivation">
  <h3>Reality Check: Why Use Version Control?</h3>
  <p>You're working on a text analysis project. You try a new approach to cleaning your data, but it makes everything worse. Without version control, what do you do?</p>

  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="vc-quiz" value="wrong1">
    Manually undo every change you can remember
  </label>

  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="vc-quiz" value="wrong2">
    Hope you saved an older copy somewhere
  </label>

  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="vc-quiz" value="correct">
    Run one command to restore the previous working version
  </label>
</div>
```

### Real DH Use Cases

Version control isn't just for software engineers. DH scholars use it for:

- **Code development:** Track changes to Python scripts for text analysis, visualization, and data processing
- **Collaboration:** Multiple researchers working on the same corpus, dataset, or digital edition
- **Digital editions:** Track revisions to TEI-encoded texts, annotations, and metadata
- **Reproducible research:** Document exactly how your analysis evolved, so others can verify and build on it

## Prerequisites

Before starting this guide, you should:

- Be comfortable with basic terminal commands (`cd`, `ls`, `mkdir`) — see the [Command Line Basics](command-line.qmd) guide
- Have Git installed — see the [Environment Setup](environment-setup.qmd) guide
- Have a GitHub account — also covered in [Environment Setup](environment-setup.qmd)

## The Git Mental Model

Git tracks your files through three states:

1. **Working Directory** — the files you see and edit on your computer
2. **Staging Area** — files you've marked as ready to save (using `git add`)
3. **Repository** — the permanent record of saved snapshots (created by `git commit`)

Think of it like packing a box:

- You make changes to files (working directory)
- You decide which changes to include (staging area — like putting items in the box)
- You seal and label the box (commit — a permanent snapshot with a message)

```
Working Directory  →  Staging Area  →  Repository
   (your files)      (git add)       (git commit)
                                          ↓
                                       GitHub
                                      (git push)
```

## Your First Git Repository

Let's create a repository from scratch.

### Step 1: Create a Project Folder

::::{.panel-tabset}

### Mac

```bash
mkdir ~/Documents/my-first-repo
cd ~/Documents/my-first-repo
```

### PC

```powershell
mkdir C:\Users\YourName\Documents\my-first-repo
cd C:\Users\YourName\Documents\my-first-repo
```

::::

### Step 2: Initialize Git

```bash
git init
```

You'll see: `Initialized empty Git repository in .../my-first-repo/.git/`

This creates a hidden `.git` folder that stores all the tracking information. You never need to touch this folder directly.

### Step 3: Create Some Files

```bash
echo "# My First Repository" > README.md
echo "This is a test file." > notes.txt
```

### Step 4: Check the Status

```bash
git status
```

Git will show your new files as "untracked" — it sees them but isn't tracking changes yet.

### Step 5: Stage and Commit

```bash
git add README.md notes.txt
git commit -m "Initial commit: add README and notes"
```

That's it. You've saved your first snapshot. The `-m` flag lets you add a message describing what you did.

## The Core Git Workflow

After the initial setup, your daily workflow is four steps:

1. **Make changes** — edit files, add new ones, delete what you don't need
2. **Review** — `git status` shows what changed; `git diff` shows the details
3. **Stage** — `git add` marks files for the next commit
4. **Commit** — `git commit -m "description"` saves a permanent snapshot

```bash
# 1. Check what's changed
git status

# 2. See the details of changes
git diff

# 3. Stage specific files
git add file1.py file2.py

# 4. Commit with a descriptive message
git commit -m "Add sentiment analysis function and test data"
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Commit Often, Commit Small
A good commit captures one logical change. "Add sentiment analysis function" is a good commit message. "Did a bunch of stuff" is not. Small, frequent commits make it easy to find and undo specific changes later.
:::

## Essential Commands Reference

| Command | What It Does |
|---------|-------------|
| `git status` | Show which files have changed |
| `git add <file>` | Stage a file for the next commit |
| `git add .` | Stage all changed files |
| `git commit -m "message"` | Save a snapshot with a description |
| `git log --oneline` | Show commit history (compact view) |
| `git diff` | Show what changed since the last commit |
| `git restore <file>` | Undo changes to a file (go back to last commit) |

## Writing Good Commit Messages

Your future self will thank you for clear commit messages.

**Bad messages:**

- `"update"`
- `"fixed stuff"`
- `"asdfasdf"`
- `"changes"`

**Good messages:**

- `"Add word frequency analysis for corpus A"`
- `"Fix encoding error in French text processing"`
- `"Remove duplicate entries from metadata CSV"`
- `"Update README with project description and setup instructions"`

**The formula:** Start with a verb (Add, Fix, Update, Remove, Refactor). Describe what changed and why in under 50 characters.

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="commit-messages">
  <h3>Commit Message Check</h3>
  <p>Which is the best commit message for adding a function that counts word frequencies?</p>

  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="commit-quiz" value="wrong1">
    "updates"
  </label>

  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="commit-quiz" value="wrong2">
    "I added a new function to count how many times each word appears in the text files and also fixed some formatting"
  </label>

  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="commit-quiz" value="correct">
    "Add word frequency counter for corpus analysis"
  </label>

  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="commit-quiz" value="wrong3">
    "word stuff"
  </label>
</div>
```

## Working with GitHub

GitHub stores your repositories online. This gives you:

- **Backup** — your work exists in two places
- **Collaboration** — share code with classmates and colleagues
- **Publishing** — host websites for free with GitHub Pages
- **Portfolio** — show your work to employers and peers

### Connecting Your Local Repository to GitHub

1. Go to [github.com](https://github.com) and click **New Repository**
2. Name it `my-first-repo` (match your local folder name)
3. **Do not** check "Add a README file" — you already have one locally
4. Click **Create Repository**
5. GitHub will show you commands to run. Use the "push an existing repository" option:

```bash
git remote add origin https://github.com/YOUR-USERNAME/my-first-repo.git
git branch -M main
git push -u origin main
```

Refresh the GitHub page — your files are now online.

### The Push/Pull Workflow

Once connected to GitHub:

```bash
# Send your local commits to GitHub
git push

# Get changes from GitHub (e.g., a collaborator's work)
git pull
```

```
Your Computer          GitHub
     ↑                   ↑
     |    git push →     |
     |                   |
     |    ← git pull     |
     ↓                   ↓
```

## Branching: Safe Experimentation

Branches let you try something new without affecting your main work. If the experiment works, you merge it in. If it doesn't, you delete the branch and nothing is lost.

```bash
# Create and switch to a new branch
git checkout -b experiment-new-analysis

# ... make changes, commit them ...

# Switch back to main
git checkout main

# If the experiment worked, merge it in
git merge experiment-new-analysis

# If it didn't, just delete the branch
git branch -d experiment-new-analysis
```

```{=html}
<div class="quiz" data-quiz="branching">
  <h3>Branching Scenario</h3>
  <p>You want to try a completely different approach to cleaning your dataset, but you're not sure it will work. What should you do?</p>

  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="branch-quiz" value="wrong1">
    Make a copy of the entire project folder
  </label>

  <label class="quiz-option" data-correct>
    <input type="radio" name="branch-quiz" value="correct">
    Create a new Git branch, experiment there, merge or delete when done
  </label>

  <label class="quiz-option">
    <input type="radio" name="branch-quiz" value="wrong2">
    Just make the changes and hope for the best
  </label>
</div>
```

## The .gitignore File

Some files shouldn't be tracked by Git: temporary files, API keys, large datasets, system files. A `.gitignore` file tells Git to skip them.

Create a file called `.gitignore` in your project root:

```text
# Python
__pycache__/
*.pyc
.ipynb_checkpoints/
venv/

# OS files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db

# Sensitive data
.env
*.key
passwords.txt

# Large data files
data/raw/*.csv
*.zip
```

::: {.callout-important}
## Never Commit Passwords or API Keys
If you accidentally commit a file with sensitive information, deleting the file in a later commit doesn't remove it from the history. Git remembers everything. Use `.gitignore` to prevent this from happening in the first place.
:::

## Recovering from Mistakes

This is where Git earns its keep.

### "I changed a file and want to undo it"

```bash
git restore filename.py
```

This reverts the file to the last committed version. Uncommitted changes are gone.

### "I staged a file by accident"

```bash
git restore --staged filename.py
```

The file is still changed, but it's no longer staged for the next commit.

### "I made a bad commit and want to undo it"

```bash
git revert HEAD
```

This creates a new commit that undoes the previous one. The history is preserved — you can see that a mistake was made and corrected.

### "I want to see what a file looked like three commits ago"

```bash
git log --oneline          # find the commit hash
git show abc1234:filename.py   # view the file at that commit
```

## A Practical DH Workflow

Here's what a typical week looks like using Git for a text analysis project:

**Monday:** Start a new analysis branch

```bash
git checkout -b sentiment-analysis
```

**Tuesday:** Make progress, commit at the end of the day

```bash
git add analysis.py data/cleaned_corpus.csv
git commit -m "Add VADER sentiment scoring for corpus"
```

**Wednesday:** Hit a dead end, try a different approach

```bash
git commit -m "WIP: attempt regex-based cleaning (not working yet)"
```

**Thursday:** Get it working, merge to main

```bash
git commit -m "Fix regex cleaning, all tests pass"
git checkout main
git merge sentiment-analysis
git push
```

## Quick Reference

### Setup (do once)

```bash
git init                              # create new repository
git clone <url>                       # copy existing repository
git config --global user.name "You"   # set your name
git config --global user.email "you@email.com"
```

### Daily commands

```bash
git status                  # what's changed?
git add <file>              # stage for commit
git commit -m "message"     # save snapshot
git push                    # send to GitHub
git pull                    # get from GitHub
```

### Branching

```bash
git checkout -b <branch>    # create and switch
git checkout main           # switch to main
git merge <branch>          # merge branch into current
git branch -d <branch>      # delete branch
```

### History and undo

```bash
git log --oneline           # compact history
git diff                    # see unstaged changes
git restore <file>          # undo file changes
git revert HEAD             # undo last commit (safely)
```

## What's Next

You now have the foundation for professional file management with Git. As you work through DH projects, you'll develop muscle memory for the add-commit-push cycle. It becomes as natural as saving a file.

For more on using the command line effectively with Git and other tools, see the [Command Line Basics](command-line.qmd) guide.

---

*Version control is the difference between "I think I had a working version somewhere" and "I know exactly when it worked and I can go back to it." Start using Git now, even for small projects. Your future self will thank you.*
